Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## - UNDERSTANDING OVER SQUASHING AND BOTTLENECKS ON GRAPHS VIA CURVATURE

**Jake Topping**[12] _[†]_ **, Francesco Di Giovanni**[3] _[†]_ **, Benjamin P. Chamberlain**[3] **, Xiaowen Dong**[1] **, and Michael M. Bronstein**[23] 1University of Oxford 2Imperial College London 3Twitter

## ABSTRACT

Most graph neural networks (GNNs) use the message passing paradigm, in which node features are propagated on the input graph. Recent works pointed to the distortion of information flowing from distant nodes as a factor limiting the efficiency of message passing for tasks relying on long-distance interactions. This phenomenon, referred to as ‘over-squashing’, has been heuristically attributed to graph bottlenecks where the number of _k_ -hop neighbors grows rapidly with _k_ . We provide a precise description of the over-squashing phenomenon in GNNs and analyze how it arises from bottlenecks in the graph. For this purpose, we introduce a new edge-based combinatorial curvature and prove that negatively curved edges are responsible for the over-squashing issue. We also propose and experimentally test a curvature-based graph rewiring method to alleviate the over-squashing.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

In the past few years, deep learning on graphs and in particular graph neural networks (GNNs) (Sperduti, 1994; Goller & Kuchler, 1996; Sperduti & Starita, 1997; Frasconi et al., 1998; Gori et al., 2005; Scarselli et al., 2008; Bruna et al., 2014; Defferrard et al., 2016; Kipf & Welling, 2017; Gilmer et al., 2017) have become very popular in the machine learning community due to their ability to deal with broad classes of systems of relations and interactions, ranging from social networks to particle physics (Shlomi et al., 2021).

The vast majority of GNNs follow the _message passing_ paradigm (Gilmer et al.,

Figure 1: Top: evolution of curvature on a surface may reduce the bottleneck. Bottom: this paper shows how the same may be done on graphs to improve GNN performance. Blue/red shows negative/positive curvature.

2017), using learnable non-linear functions to diffuse information on the graph. Multiple popular GNN architectures such as GCN (Kipf & Welling, 2017) and GAT (Veliˇckovi´c et al., 2018) can be posed as particular flavors of this scheme and considered instances of a more general framework of geometric deep learning (Bronstein et al., 2021).

Some of the drawbacks of the message passing paradigm have now been identified and formalized, including the limits of expressive power (Xu et al., 2019; Morris et al., 2019; Maron et al., 2019) and the problem of over-smoothing (NT & Maehara, 2019; Oono & Suzuki, 2020). On the other hand, much less is known about the phenomenon of _over-squashing_ , consisting in the distortion of messages being propagated from distant nodes. Alon & Yahav (2021) proposed rewiring the graph as a way of reducing the _bottleneck_ , defined as those topological properties in the graph leading to over-squashing. This approach is in line with multiple other results e.g. using connectivity diffusion (Klicpera et al., 2019) as a preprocessing step to facilitate graph learning. Yet, the exact understanding of the over-squashing and how it originates from the bottlenecks in the topology of the underlying

graph are still elusive. Consequently, there is currently no consensus on the right method (either based on graph rewiring or not) to address the bottleneck and hence alleviate the over-squashing.

In this paper, we address these questions using tools from differential geometry, which traditionally is concerned with the study of manifolds. It offers an appealing framework to study the properties of graphs, in particular arguing that graphs, like manifolds, exhibit _curvature_ that makes them more suitable to be realized in spaces with hyperbolic geometry (Liu et al., 2019; Chami et al., 2019; Boguna et al., 2021). One notion of curvature that has received attention for graph learning is _Ricci curvature_ (Hamilton, 1988), also known in geometry for its use in Ricci flow and the subsequent proof of the Poincaré conjecture (Perelman, 2003). Certain graph analogues of the Ricci curvature (Forman, 2003; Ollivier, 2009; Sreejith et al., 2016) were used in Ni et al. (2018) for a discrete version of Ricci flow to construct a metric between graphs. Graph Ricci flow was also used in Ni et al. (2019) for community detection. Both of these methods use the edge weights as a substitute for the metric of a manifold, and do not change the topological structure of the graph.

**Contributions and Outline.** This paper, to our knowledge, is the first theoretical study of the bottleneck and over-squashing phenomena in message passing neural networks from a geometric perspective. In Section 2, we propose the Jacobian of node representations as a formal way of measuring the over-squashing and we show that the graph topology may compromise message propagation in graph neural networks by creating a bottleneck. In Section 3, we investigate how such a bottleneck is induced which leads to the over-squashing of information. To this aim, we introduce a new combinatorial edge-based curvature called Balanced Forman curvature that constitutes a sharp lower bound to the standard Ollivier curvature on graphs, and prove that negatively curved edges are responsible for the formation of bottlenecks (and hence for over-squashing). In Section 4, we present a new curvature-based method for graph rewiring called Stochastic Discrete Ricci Flow. According to the theoretical results in Section 3, this rewiring method is suited to address the graph bottleneck and hence alleviate the over-squashing by surgically targeting the edges responsible for the issue. By contrast, we rigorously show that a recently introduced diffusion-based rewiring scheme might generally fail to reduce the bottleneck. Finally, in Section 5, we compare different rewiring strategies experimentally on several standard graph learning datasets.

## 2.2 THE OVER-SQUASHING PROBLEM

Multiple recent papers observed that MPNNs tend to perform poorly in situations when the learned task requires long-range dependencies and at the same time the structure of the graph results in exponentially many long-range neighboring nodes. We say that a graph learning problem has _long-range dependencies_ when the output of a MPNN depends on representations of distant nodes interacting with each other. If long-range dependencies are present, messages coming from nonadjacent nodes need to be propagated across the network without being too distorted. In many cases however (e.g. in ‘small-world’ graphs such as social networks), the size of the receptive field _Br_ ( _i_ ) grows exponentially with _r_ . If this occurs, representations of exponentially many neighboring nodes need to be compressed into fixed-size vectors to propagate messages to node _i_ , causing a phenomenon referred to as _over-squashing_ of information (Alon & Yahav, 2021). In line with Alon & Yahav (2021), we refer to those structural properties of the graph that lead to over-squashing as a _bottleneck_[1] .

The sensitivity analysis in Lemma 1 relates the over-squashing – as measured by the Jacobian of the node representations – to the graph topology via powers of the augmented normalized adjacency matrix. In the next section we explore this connection further by analyzing which local properties of the graph structure affect the right hand side in equation 2, hence causing the bottleneck. We will address this problem by introducing a new combinatorial notion of edge-based curvature and showing that _negatively curved edges are those responsible for the over-squashing phenomenon_ .

## 3 GRAPH CURVATURE AND BOTTLENECK

A natural object in Riemannian geometry is the _Ricci curvature_ , a bilinear form determining the geodesic dispersion, i.e. whether geodesics starting at nearby points with ‘same’ velocity remain parallel ( _Euclidean space_ ), converge ( _spherical space_ ), or diverge ( _hyperbolic space_ ). To motivate the introduction of a Ricci curvature for graphs, we focus on these three cases. Consider two nodes _i ∼ j_ and two edges starting at _i_ and _j_ respectively. In a discrete spherical geometry (Figure 2a), the edges would meet at _k_ to form a triangle (complete graph). In a discrete Euclidean geometry (Figure 2b), the edges would stay parallel and form a 4-cycle based at _i ∼ j_ (orthogonal grid). Finally, in a discrete hyperbolic geometry (Figure 2c), the mutual distance of the edge endpoints would have grown compared to that of _i_ and _j_ (tree). Therefore, a Ricci curvature for graphs should provide us with more sophisticated tools than the degree to analyze the neighborhood of an edge.

The over-squashing phenomenon instead may occur even in deep GNNs with the number of layers larger than the graph diameter, as tested experimentally in Alon & Yahav (2021).

**Curvature and over-squashing.** Thanks to our new combinatorial curvature and the sensitivity analysis in Lemma 1, we are able to relate local curvature properties to the Jacobian of the node representations. This leads to one of the main results of this paper: **negatively curved edges are those causing the graph bottleneck and thus leading to the over-squashing phenomenon:**

**Theorem 4.** _Consider a MPNN as in equation 1. Let i ∼ j with di ≤ dj and assume that:_

- _(i) |∇φℓ| ≤ α and |∇ψℓ| ≤ β for each_ 0 _≤ ℓ ≤ L −_ 1 _, with L ≥_ 2 _the depth of the MPNN._
_Then there exists Qj ⊂ S_ 2( _i_ ) _satisfying |Qj| > δ[−]_[1] _and for_ 0 _≤ ℓ_ 0 _≤ L −_ 2 _we have_

Condition (i) is always satisfied and allows us to control the message passing functions. The requirement (ii) instead means that the curvature of ( _i, j_ ) is negative enough when compared to the degrees of _i_ and _j_ (recall that Ric( _i, j_ ) _> −_ 2). The further condition on _γ_ max is to avoid pathological cases where we have a large number of degenerate 4-cycles passing through the same three nodes.

To understand the conclusions in Theorem 4, let us fix _ℓ_ 0 = 0. The equation 4 shows that negatively curved edges are the ones causing bottlenecks, interpreted as how the graph topology prevents a representation _h_[(2)] _k_ to be affected by _non_ -adjacent features _h_[(0)] _i_ = _xi_ . Theorem 4 implies that if we have a negatively curved edge as in (ii), then there exist a large number of nodes _k_ such that GNNs - on average - struggle to propagate messages from _i_ to _k_ in two layers despite these nodes _k_ being at distance 2 from _i_ . In this case the over-squashing occurs as measured by the Jacobian in equation 4 and hence the propagation of information suffers. If the task at hand has long-range dependencies, then the over-squashing caused by the negatively curved edges may compromise the performance.

**Bottleneck via Cheeger constant.** We now relate the previous discussion about bottlenecks and curvature to spectral properties of the graph. In particular, since the spectral gap of a graph can be interpreted as a topological obstruction to the graph being partitioned into two communities, we argue below that this quantity is related to the graph bottleneck and should hence be controllable by the curvature. We start with an intuitive explanation: suppose we are given a graph _G_ with two communities separated by few edges. In this case, we see that the graph can be easily disconnected. This property is encoded in the classical notion of the _Cheeger constant_ (Chung & Graham, 1997)

where _λ_ 1 is the first non-zero eigenvalue of the normalized graph Laplacian, often referred to as the _spectral gap_ . A graph with two tightly connected communities ( _S_ and _V \ S_ := _S_[¯] ) and few inter-community edges has a small Cheeger constant _hG_ . For nodes in different communities to interact with each other, all messages need to go through the same few _bridges_ hence leading to the over-squashing of information (a similar intuition was explored in Alon & Yahav (2021)). Therefore, _hG_ can be interpreted as a rough measure of graph ‘bottleneckedness’, in the sense that the smaller its value, the more likely the over-squashing is to occur across inter-community edges. Since Theorem 4 implies that negatively curved edges induce the bottleneck, we expect a relationship between _hG_ and the curvature of the graph. The next proposition follows from Theorem 2 and Lin et al. (2011):

**Proposition 5.** _If_ Ric( _i, j_ ) _≥ k >_ 0 _for all i ∼ j, then λ_ 1 _/_ 2 _≥ hG ≥[k]_ 2 _[.]_

Therefore, a positive lower bound on the curvature gives us a control on _hG_ and hence on the spectral gap of the graph. In the next section, we show that diffusion-based graph-rewiring methods might fail to significantly alter _hG_ and hence correct the graph bottleneck potentially induced by inter-community edges. This will lead us to propose an alternative curvature-based graph rewiring.

## 4 CURVATURE-BASED REWIRING METHODS

The traditional paradigm of message passing graph neural networks assumes that messages are propagated on the input graph (Gilmer et al., 2017). More recently, there is a trend to decouple the input graph from the graph used for information propagation. This can take the form of graph subsampling or resampling to deal with scalability (Hamilton et al., 2017) or topological noise (Zhang et al., 2019), using larger motif-based (Monti et al., 2018) or multi-hop filters (Rossi et al., 2020), or changing the graph either as a preprocessing step (Klicpera et al., 2019; Alon & Yahav, 2021) or adaptively for the downstream task (Wang et al., 2019; Kazi et al., 2020). Such methods are often generically referred to as _graph rewiring_ .

In the context of this paper, we assume that graph rewiring attempts to produce a new graph _G[′]_ = ( _V, E[′]_ ) with a different edge structure that reduces the bottleneck and hence potentially alleviates the over-squashing of information. We propose a method that leverages the graph curvature to guide the rewiring steps in a surgical way by modifying the negatively-curved edges, so to decrease the bottleneck without significantly compromising the statistical properties of the input graph. We also rigorously show that a random-walk based rewiring method might generally fail to obtain an edge set _E[′]_ with a significant improvement in its bottleneckedness as measured by the Cheeger constant.

**Curvature-based graph rewiring.** Since according to Theorem 4 negatively curved edges induce a bottleneck and are hence responsible for over-squashing, a curvature-based rewiring method should attempt to alleviate a graph’s strongly-negatively curved edges. To this end we implement a simple rewiring method called Stochastic Discrete Ricci Flow (SDRF), described in Algorithm 1.

## **Algorithm 1:** Stochastic Discrete Ricci Flow (SDRF)

**Input:** graph _G_ , temperature _τ >_ 0, max number of iterations, optional Ric upper-bound _C_[+] **Repeat**

1) For edge _i ∼ j_ with minimal Ricci curvature Ric( _i, j_ ):

Calculate vector _**x**_ where _xkl_ = Ric _kl_ ( _i, j_ ) _−_ Ric( _i, j_ ), the improvement to Ric( _i, j_ ) from adding edge _k ∼ l_ where _k ∈ B_ 1( _i_ ), _l ∈ B_ 1( _j_ ); Sample index _k, l_ with probability softmax( _τ_ _**x**_ ) _kl_ and add edge _k ∼ l_ to _G_ .
2) Remove edge _i ∼ j_ with maximal Ricci curvature Ric( _i, j_ ) if Ric( _i, j_ ) _> C_[+] . **Until** convergence, or max iterations reached;

At each iteration this preprocessing step adds an edge to ‘support’ the graph’s most negatively curved edge, and then removes the most positively curved edge. The requirement on the added edge _k ∼ l_ that _k ∈ B_ 1( _i_ ) and _l ∈ B_ 1( _j_ ) ensures that we’re adding either an extra 3- or 4-cycle around the negative edge _i ∼ j_ so that this is a local modification. The graph edit distance between the original and preprocessed graph is bounded above by 2 _×_ the max number of iterations. The temperature _τ_ determines how stochastic the edge addition is, with _τ_ = _∞_ being fully deterministic (the best edge is always added). At each step we remove the edge with most positive curvature to balance the distributions of curvature and node degrees. We use Balanced Forman curvature as in equation 3 for Ric( _i, j_ ). _C_[+] can be chosen to stop the method skewing the curvature distribution negative, including _C_[+] = _∞_ to not remove any edges. The method is inspired by the continuous (backwards) Ricci flow with the aim of homogenizing edge curvatures. This is different from more direct extensions of Ricci flow on graphs where it becomes increasingly expensive to propagate messages across negatively curved edges (as in other applications such as Ni et al. (2019)). An example alongside its continuous analogue can be seen in Figure 1.

**Can random-walk based rewiring address bottlenecks?** A good way of understanding the effectiveness of SDRF in reducing the graph bottleneck is through comparison with random-walk based rewiring strategies. Recall that, as argued in Section 3, the Cheeger constant _hG_ of a graph constitutes a rough measure of its bottleneckedness as induced by the inter-community edges (a small _hG_ is indicative of a bottleneck). Suppose we are given a graph _G_ with a small _hG_ and wish to rewire it into a graph _G[′]_ with a significantly improved Cheeger constant in order to reduce the inter-community bottleneck. A random-walk based rewiring method such as DIGL (Klicpera et al., 2019) acts by smoothing out the graph adjacency and hence tends to promote connections among nodes at short

The property that the new Cheeger constant is directly controlled by the old one stems from the fact that a random-walk approach like in Klicpera et al. (2019) is meant to act more relevantly on intra-community edges rather than inter-community edges because it prioritizes short diffusion distance nodes. This is also why this method performs well on high-homophily datasets, as discussed below. In particular, for a fixed _α ∈_ (0 _,_ 1), the bound in Theorem 6 can be very small. As a specific example, consider two complete graphs _Kn_ joined by one bridge. Then _hG_ = ( _n_ ( _n −_ 1) + 1) _[−]_[1] , which means that the bound on the right hand side is _O_ ( _n[−]_[2] ).

Theorem 6 implies that a diffusion approach such as DIGL might fail to yield a new edge set _E[′]_ with a sufficiently improved bottleneck. By contrast, from Theorem 4 and Proposition 5, we deduce that a curvature-based rewiring method such as SDRF properly addresses the edges that cause the bottleneck.

**Graph structure preservation.** Although a graph-rewiring approach aims at providing a new edge set _E[′]_ potentially more beneficial for the given learning task, it is still desirable to control how far _E[′]_ is from _E_ . In this regard, we note that a curvature-based rewiring is surgical in nature and hence more likely to preserve the structure of the input graph better than a random-walk based approach. Consider, for example, that we are given _ρ >_ 0 and wish to rewire the graph such that the new edge set _E[′]_ is within graph-edit distance _ρ_ from the original _E_ . Theorem 4 tells us how to do the rewiring under such constraints in order to best address the over-squashing: the topological modifications need to be localized _around the most negatively-curved edges_ . We can do this with SDRF, with the maximum number of iterations set to _ρ/_ 2.

Secondly, we also point out that Ric( _i, j_ ) _< −_ 2 + _δ_ implies that min _{di, dj} >_ 2 _/δ_ . Therefore, if we mostly modify the edge set at those nodes _i, j_ joined by an edge with large negative curvature, then we are perturbing nodes with high degree where such a change is relatively insignificant, and thus overall statistical properties of the rewired graph such as degree distribution are likely to be better preserved. Moreover, graph convolutional networks tend to be more stable to perturbations of high degree nodes (Zügner et al., 2020; Kenlay et al., 2021), making curvature-based rewiring more suitable for the downstream learning tasks with popular GNN architectures.

**Homophily and bottleneck.** As a final remark, note that the graph rewiring techniques considered in this paper (both DIGL and SDRF) are based purely on the topological structure of the graph and completely agnostic to the node features and to whether the dataset is _homophilic_ (adjacent nodes have same labels) or _heterophilic_ . Nonetheless, the different nature of these rewiring methods allows us to draw a few broad conclusions about their suitability in each of these settings. A random-walk

## 5 EXPERIMENTAL RESULTS

**Experiment setup.** To demonstrate the theoretical results in this paper we ran a suite of semisupervised node classification tasks comparing our curvature-based rewiring method SDRF to DIGL from Klicpera et al. (2019) (GDC with the PPR kernel) and the +FA method from Alon & Yahav (2021), where the last layer of the GNN is made fully connected. We evaluate the methods on nine datasets: Cornell, Texas and Wisconsin from the WebKB dataset[4] ; Chameleon and Squirrel (Rozemberczki et al., 2021) along with Actor (Tang et al., 2009); and Cora (McCallum et al., 2000), Citeseer (Sen et al., 2008) and Pubmed (Namata et al., 2012). Statistics for these datasets can be found in Appendix F.1. Our base model is a GCN (Kipf & Welling, 2017). Following Shchur et al. (2018) and Klicpera et al. (2019) we optimized hyperparameters for all dataset-preprocessing combinations separately by random search over 100 data splits. Results are reported as average accuracies on a test set used once with 95% confidence intervals calculated by bootstrapping. We compared the performance on graphs with no preprocessing, making the graph undirected, +FA, DIGL, SDRF, and the given combinations. For DIGL + Undirected we symmetrized the diffusion matrix as in Klicpera et al. (2019), and for SDRF + Undirected we made the graph undirected before applying SDRF. For more details on the experiments and datasets see Appendix F, and for the hyperparameters used for each model and preprocessing see Appendix F.4.

(2019) (restated in Appendix F, equation 21), by which we can see our experiment set is diverse with respect to homophily. We see that SDRF improves upon the baseline in all cases, and that the largest improvements are seen on the low-homophily datasets. We also see that SDRF matches or outperforms DIGL and +FA on most datasets, supporting our argument that curvature-based rewiring is a viable candidate for improving GNN performance.

**Graph topology change.** Furthermore, SDRF preserves the graph topology to a far greater extent than DIGL due to its surgical nature. Table 3 shows the number of edges added / removed by the two preprocessings on each dataset as a percentage of the original number of edges. We see that for optimal performance DIGL makes the graph much denser, which significantly affects the node degrees and may negatively impact the time and space complexity of the downstream GNN, which typically are _O_ ( _E_ ). ## 6 CONCLUSION

In this paper, we studied the graph bottleneck and the over-squashing phenomena limiting the performance of message passing graph neural networks from a geometric perspective. We started with a Jacobian approach to determine how the over-squashing phenomenon is dictated by the graph topology as in equation 2. We then investigated further how the topology induces the bottleneck and hence causes over-squashing. We introduced a new notion of edge-based Ricci curvature called Balanced Forman curvature, relating it to the classical Ollivier curvature (Theorem 2). We then proved in Theorem 4 that negatively-curved edges are responsible for over-squashing, calling for a possibility of curvature-based rewiring of the graph in order to improve its bottleneckedness. We show one such possibility (Algorithm 1), inspired by the classical Ricci flow and comment on the advantages of surgical method such as this. We show both theoretically and experimentally that the proposed method can be advantageous compared to a diffusion-based rewiring approach, opening the door for curvature-based rewiring methods for improving GNN performance going forward.

**Limitations and future directions.** Our paper establishes a geometric perspective on the graph bottleneck and over-squashing, providing new tools to study and cope with these phenomena. One limitation of our work is that the theoretical results presented here do not currently extend to multigraphs. In addition, the current methodology is agnostic to information beyond the graph topology, such as node features. In future works, we will develop a notion of the curvature and the corresponding rewiring method that can take into account such information.

progressive__claude-opus-4.7.label: Progressive (claude-opus-4.7)
progressive__claude-opus-4.7.overall_feedback: This paper presents a theoretically grounded and timely contribution to understanding over-squashing in GNNs, an important but under-analyzed phenomenon. The strengths are clear: the authors formalize over-squashing via the Jacobian of node representations, introduce a novel Balanced Forman curvature with a principled connection to Ollivier-Ricci curvature, and propose a curvature-based rewiring method (Stochastic Discrete Ricci Flow) that is both theoretically motivated and experimentally validated. The geometric framing—drawing analogies to Ricci flow and the Poincaré conjecture—is compelling and situates the work within an active line of research on geometric deep learning. However, several issues warrant attention. First, the introduction is dense and assumes substantial background in both GNNs and differential geometry; a more accessible motivating example early on would help readers unfamiliar with Ricci curvature appreciate why it is the right tool for this problem, as opposed to simpler spectral or combinatorial bottleneck measures (e.g., Cheeger constants). Second, the relationship between over-squashing and over-smoothing—two phenomena that are often conflated in the literature—is not clearly delineated upfront, which could cause confusion. Third, while the authors claim their rewiring "surgically targets" problematic edges and contrast it favorably with diffusion-based approaches like DIGL, the introduction promises a rigorous demonstration that diffusion rewiring "might generally fail," which is a strong claim whose scope and practical implications need careful qualification. Finally, the writing contains minor typographical issues (e.g., inconsistent spacing, the awkward title formatting) and the abstract could more concretely preview the empirical gains. Overall, this appears to be a substantive and novel contribution, but its accessibility and the precise boundaries of its theoretical claims will be key to evaluate in the remainder of the paper.

progressive__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper is ambitious, timely, and potentially significant, offering a compelling geometric perspective on over-squashing in message-passing GNNs and proposing a principled link between graph bottlenecks, curvature, and rewiring. The motivation is clearly articulated, the introduction situates the work well relative to expressivity, over-smoothing, diffusion rewiring, and discrete Ricci curvature, and the stated contributions suggest a strong combination of theory and empirical validation. The main strength is the attempt to move beyond heuristic notions of bottlenecks by formalizing over-squashing through Jacobians and connecting it to an edge-based curvature notion, which could provide a useful diagnostic and intervention mechanism. That said, the beginning is dense and mathematically heavy, and some readers may struggle to distinguish early on which claims are definitions, intuitions, or rigorously established results; the paper would benefit from clearer informal explanations of the key mechanism before introducing technical machinery. The most significant issues to watch for are whether the proposed Balanced Forman curvature is genuinely well justified beyond being analytically convenient, how tight and practically meaningful the theoretical connection is between negative curvature and over-squashing, and whether the rewiring method improves performance for the claimed reason rather than through generic connectivity augmentation. The paper should also be careful about the strength of causal language such as “negatively curved edges are responsible,” since over-squashing can depend on architecture, features, normalization, depth, and task structure as well as topology. Overall, the work appears high quality and conceptually original, but its impact depends on the rigor of the later proofs, the interpretability of the curvature measure, and the extent to which experiments isolate the proposed mechanism from simpler graph densification or diffusion baselines.

comments.title: The MPNN formulation appears more restrictive than “generic” suggests
comments.quote:
Given a family of message functions _ψℓ_ : R _[p][ℓ] ×_ R _[p][ℓ] →_ R _[p] ℓ[′]_ and update functions _φℓ_ : R _[p][ℓ] ×_ R _[p] ℓ[′] →_ R _[p][ℓ]_[+1] , we can write the ( _ℓ_ + 1)-st layer output of a generic MPNN as follows (Gilmer et al., 2017):

Here we have used the augmented normalized adjacency matrix to propagate messages from each node to its neighbors, which simply leads to a degree normalization of the message functions _ψℓ_ .
comments.explanation: The setup is clear for GCN-like message passing with fixed symmetrically normalized adjacency, and later sensitivity bounds depend on powers of this matrix. However, calling it a “generic MPNN” is too broad. In the Gilmer et al. framework, aggregation need not use fixed symmetric degree normalization; variants may use unnormalized sums, means, attention weights, edge features, or learned edge-dependent weights. One could absorb \(\hat A_{ij}\) into an edge-dependent message function, but the displayed \(\psi_\ell\) only takes node representations as arguments. The formulation is therefore better described as a normalized message-passing subclass, and the later topology/Jacobian analysis applies directly to that subclass rather than to all MPNNs.

comments.title: Possible overstatement about prior Ricci-flow methods not changing graph topology
comments.quote: Graph Ricci flow was also used in Ni et al. (2019) for community detection. Both of these methods use the edge weights as a substitute for the metric of a manifold, and do not change the topological structure of the graph.
comments.explanation: The intended contrast is that earlier graph Ricci-flow work evolves edge weights, whereas the proposed method changes the edge set. But the statement is somewhat categorical. In community-detection uses of Ricci flow, evolved edge weights may be followed by thresholding, surgery, or removal of bridge edges to reveal communities. If the authors mean only that the Ricci-flow update equation itself keeps the incidence structure fixed, that should be stated more narrowly.

comments.title: Stated value of \((\hat A^{r+1})_{is}\) appears inconsistent with a binary-tree neighborhood
comments.quote: For example, if _dG_ ( _i, s_ ) = _r_ + 1 and the sub-graph induced on _Br_ +1( _i_ ) is a binary tree, then ( _A_[ˆ] _[r]_[+1] ) _is_ = 2 _[−]_[1] 3 _[−][r]_ , which gives an exponential decay of the node dependence on input features at distance _r_ , as also heuristically argued by Alon & Yahav (2021).
comments.explanation: The qualitative exponential-decay point is correct, but the displayed coefficient seems inconsistent with a binary tree under the earlier definition \(\hat A=\tilde D^{-1/2}\tilde A\tilde D^{-1/2}\). For a length-\(r+1\) geodesic, \((\hat A^{r+1})_{is}\) is the product of normalized edge weights along the unique path. The value \(2^{-1}3^{-r}\) matches an augmented normalized path with endpoint augmented degrees 2 and internal augmented degrees 3, not a rooted binary tree, whose augmented degrees would typically differ. The main exponential conclusion survives, but the specific coefficient/base appears incorrect unless “binary tree” is being used in a nonstandard path-like sense.

comments.title: Ricci curvature motivation conflates Ricci with sectional curvature/geodesic deviation
comments.quote: A natural object in Riemannian geometry is the _Ricci curvature_ , a bilinear form determining the geodesic dispersion, i.e. whether geodesics starting at nearby points with ‘same’ velocity remain parallel ( _Euclidean space_ ), converge ( _spherical space_ ), or diverge ( _hyperbolic space_ ).
comments.explanation: As intuition for graph Ricci curvature, the sentence is understandable. Strictly speaking, however, pairwise geodesic deviation in a specified two-dimensional direction is governed by sectional curvature via the Jacobi equation. Ricci curvature is a trace or average of sectional curvatures and controls volume distortion or averaged focusing, not arbitrary geodesic dispersion in full generality. In two-dimensional constant-curvature examples the distinction collapses, so the issue is not fatal, but the wording overstates what Ricci curvature itself determines.

comments.title: Qualitative sign description of Balanced Forman curvature may overstate the definition
comments.quote: The curvature is negative when _i ∼ j_ behaves as a _bridge_ between _S_ 1( _i_ ) and _S_ 1( _j_ ), while it is positive when _S_ 1( _i_ ) and _S_ 1( _j_ ) stay connected after removing _i ∼ j_ .
comments.explanation: The sentence captures useful intuition, but it can be read as an exact graph-theoretic sign criterion. Balanced Forman curvature depends on degrees, triangle counts, 4-cycle contributions, and the degeneracy factor \(\gamma_{\max}\), and the definition sets \(\mathrm{Ric}(i,j)=0\) when \(\min\{d_i,d_j\}=1\). Thus a bridge incident to a leaf is not negative under the given definition, and connectivity between \(S_1(i)\) and \(S_1(j)\) after deleting the edge need not imply positive curvature unless it appears through the local structures counted by the formula.

comments.title: Cheeger inequality direction in Proposition 5 appears reversed
comments.quote: **Proposition 5.** _If_ Ric( _i, j_ ) _≥ k >_ 0 _for all i ∼ j, then λ_ 1 _/_ 2 _≥ hG ≥[k]_ 2 _[.]_
comments.explanation: With the Cheeger constant defined as edge boundary divided by volume, the standard normalized-Laplacian Cheeger inequality is \(\lambda_1/2 \le h_G \le \sqrt{2\lambda_1}\), not \(\lambda_1/2 \ge h_G\). Combining a spectral-gap lower bound \(\lambda_1\ge k\) with Cheeger gives \(h_G\ge \lambda_1/2\ge k/2\), or at least the desired lower bound \(h_G\ge k/2\). The displayed inequality is false for standard examples such as complete graphs, so this appears to be a genuine order error.

comments.title: Bounded-gradient assumption is overstated as automatic
comments.quote: Condition (i) is always satisfied and allows us to control the message passing functions.
comments.explanation: The theorem assumes \(|\nabla \phi_\ell|\le \alpha\) and \(|\nabla \psi_\ell|\le \beta\). This is reasonable for many standard neural-network layers with Lipschitz activations, finite weights, and perhaps bounded feature domains, but it is not automatic for arbitrary differentiable MPNNs. Differentiability alone does not imply globally bounded derivatives. The prose should describe the condition as mild or commonly satisfied under standard architectural assumptions, not as always satisfied.

comments.title: Theorem 4 is summarized more strongly than its stated hypotheses support
comments.quote: We then proved in Theorem 4 that negatively-curved edges are responsible for over-squashing, calling for a possibility of curvature-based rewiring of the graph in order to improve its bottleneckedness.
comments.explanation: The paper’s conceptual connection between negative local curvature and bottleneck-like message compression is useful, but the theorem is more conditional than this summary suggests. Theorem 4 applies to edges satisfying sufficiently strong negative-curvature conditions relative to degrees, assumes bounded message/update derivatives, includes an additional condition on \(\gamma_{\max}\), and concludes the existence of distance-two nodes with a small average Jacobian bound. It supports the claim that certain strongly negatively curved edges can induce local over-squashing, but it does not prove that all negatively curved edges are responsible for over-squashing or that negative curvature is necessary in every graph/task setting.

comments.title: SDRF candidate edge set is underspecified for a simple graph
comments.quote: Calculate vector _**x**_ where _xkl_ = Ric _kl_ ( _i, j_ ) _−_ Ric( _i, j_ ), the improvement to Ric( _i, j_ ) from adding edge _k ∼ l_ where _k ∈ B_ 1( _i_ ), _l ∈ B_ 1( _j_ ); Sample index _k, l_ with probability softmax( _τ_ _**x**_ ) _kl_ and add edge _k ∼ l_ to _G_ .
comments.explanation: Because the graph is assumed simple, the algorithm should specify admissible candidate pairs more precisely. As written, \(k\in B_1(i)\), \(l\in B_1(j)\) may include the existing pair \((i,j)\), already-adjacent pairs, or even \(k=l\) when neighborhoods overlap. Adding such a pair would create a duplicate edge, a self-loop, or no topological change, conflicting with the simple-graph setting and with the claim that the step adds a 3- or 4-cycle. The intended candidate set is probably distinct nonadjacent endpoints excluding trivial choices, but this is not explicit enough to reproduce the method unambiguously.

comments.title: SDRF is not specified for directed datasets despite the theory being undirected
comments.quote: We compared the performance on graphs with no preprocessing, making the graph undirected, +FA, DIGL, SDRF, and the given combinations. For DIGL + Undirected we symmetrized the diffusion matrix as in Klicpera et al. (2019), and for SDRF + Undirected we made the graph undirected before applying SDRF.
comments.explanation: The theoretical development of Balanced Forman curvature and SDRF is for simple, undirected graphs. But the experimental table includes separate “Undirected” and “SDRF + Undirected” rows, suggesting that plain SDRF may be applied before symmetrization on directed datasets. It is then unclear how Ricci curvature is computed on directed edges, whether candidate additions are directed or undirected, and how edge removals are handled when only one direction exists. If SDRF is computed on the underlying undirected graph while message passing remains directed, that should be stated.

comments.title: Claims about low homophily, long-range dependencies, and label connectivity are stronger than supported
comments.quote:
In fact, long-range dependencies are often more relevant in low-homophily settings, where nodes sharing the same labels are in general not neighbors. This observation is largely confirmed by experimental results reported in the next section.

Conversely, on a low-homophily dataset, a curvature-based approach as SDRF modifies the edge set mainly around the most negatively curved edges, meaning that it decreases the bottleneck without significantly increasing the connectivity among nodes with different labels.
comments.explanation: The intuition is plausible, but the claims go beyond what is established. Low homophily does not by itself imply that the task depends on long-range same-label interactions; many heterophilic benchmarks can involve local structural or class-disassortative signals. Also, SDRF is label-agnostic, and local neighborhoods around negatively curved edges in low-homophily graphs may contain many cross-class pairs, so adding triangles or 4-cycles can still increase inter-class connectivity. Accuracy improvements after rewiring do not directly measure long-range dependence or prove that SDRF avoids adding cross-label edges.

comments.title: Evaluation protocol is ambiguous around data splits, test use, and bootstrapped intervals
comments.quote: Following Shchur et al. (2018) and Klicpera et al. (2019) we optimized hyperparameters for all dataset-preprocessing combinations separately by random search over 100 data splits. Results are reported as average accuracies on a test set used once with 95% confidence intervals calculated by bootstrapping.
comments.explanation: The wording leaves the sampling unit unclear. If hyperparameters are optimized over 100 random splits, are reported averages computed over those same splits, over independent retrainings on one final split, or over bootstrap resamples of nodes in a single test set? If confidence intervals are bootstrapped over test nodes in a transductive graph, independence assumptions are questionable; if they are bootstrapped over splits or runs, that should be stated. Since several reported accuracy differences are small, the exact protocol matters.

comments.title: Claim that SDRF adds and removes similar numbers of edges is not uniformly supported by Table 3
comments.quote: In comparison, SDRF adds and removes a similar number of edges and approximately preserves the degree distribution.
comments.explanation: The broad contrast with DIGL is fair: Table 3 indicates that DIGL often densifies graphs much more than SDRF. However, the stronger claim that SDRF adds and removes similar numbers of edges is not true across all datasets. For example, Cornell reports 7.8% added versus 33.3% removed, Texas 2.4% versus 10.4%, Wisconsin 1.4% versus 7.5%, and Actor 5.4% versus 9.9%. On several larger datasets the percentages are close, but the prose overgeneralizes.

comments.title: Observed Jacobian increases are over-attributed to Theorem 4’s upper bound
comments.quote: network’s Jacobian between a node’s prediction and the features of its 2-hop neighbors (encoded as node colors) are increased over the graph, which we may attribute to both DIGL and SDRF’s ability to alleviate the upper bound presented in Theorem 4 and thus reduce over-squashing.
comments.explanation: Visualizing Jacobians is reasonable given the paper’s use of sensitivity as a proxy for over-squashing. But observing larger trained Jacobians after preprocessing does not show that Theorem 4’s upper bound was the active constraint, nor that the increase was caused specifically by curvature/bottleneck relief rather than changed normalization, retraining dynamics, altered degrees, or other graph changes. The phrasing is somewhat cautious, but the causal link to reducing over-squashing is stronger than the visualization alone justifies.

comments.title: The claimed theoretical advantage of SDRF over diffusion rewiring is broader than what appears to be proved
comments.quote: We show both theoretically and experimentally that the proposed method can be advantageous compared to a diffusion-based rewiring approach, opening the door for curvature-based rewiring methods for improving GNN performance going forward.
comments.explanation: The theory motivates SDRF by connecting negative curvature with local Jacobian bottlenecks and by identifying limitations of diffusion/PPR-style rewiring for bottleneck measures. The experiments may show that SDRF is sometimes advantageous. However, there does not appear to be a formal guarantee that SDRF improves the Cheeger constant, improves curvature globally, reduces the Jacobian bound, or outperforms diffusion rewiring under specified conditions. The conclusion would be more precise if it said SDRF is theoretically motivated by the curvature analysis and empirically advantageous over diffusion baselines, rather than implying a direct theoretical proof of superiority.

Given a family of message functions _ψℓ_ : R _[p][ℓ] ×_ R _[p][ℓ] →_ R _[p] ℓ[′]_ and update functions _φℓ_ : R _[p][ℓ] ×_ R _[p] ℓ[′] →_ R _[p][ℓ]_[+1] , we can write the ( _ℓ_ + 1)-st layer output of a generic MPNN as follows (Gilmer et al., 2017):

comments.explanation: The intended setup is clear: the authors want a message-passing model whose propagation weights are given by the symmetrically normalized augmented adjacency matrix, because later sensitivity bounds depend on powers of this matrix. That is a standard and useful abstraction for GCN-like architectures. However, I do not think the word “generic” is quite justified as stated. In the Gilmer et al. One could absorb the factor \(\hat A_{ij}\) into an edge-dependent message function, but the displayed function type for \(\psi_\ell\) only takes node representations as arguments, not degrees or edge attributes. comments.title: Possible overstatement about prior Ricci-flow methods not changing graph topology
comments.quote: Graph Ricci flow was also used in Ni et al. (2019) for community detection. Both of these methods use the edge weights as a substitute for the metric of a manifold, and do not change the topological structure of the graph.
comments.explanation: I understand the contrast the authors are drawing: earlier graph Ricci-flow work primarily evolves edge weights, whereas their proposed rewiring changes the edge set itself. That distinction is broadly useful. Still, the statement as written may be too categorical, especially for community-detection uses of Ricci flow, where edge weights are evolved and then thresholding/surgery or removal of high-weight bridge edges can be used to reveal communities. If the authors mean that the Ricci-flow update equation itself keeps the vertex/edge incidence structure fixed, that should be stated more narrowly. Otherwise, a reader familiar with Ricci-flow community-detection pipelines could reasonably object that topology-changing post-processing is part of the method in some presentations.

comments.title: Stated value of \((\hat A^{r+1})_{is}\) appears inconsistent with a binary-tree neighborhood
comments.quote: For example, if _dG_ ( _i, s_ ) = _r_ + 1 and the sub-graph induced on _Br_ +1( _i_ ) is a binary tree, then ( _A_[ˆ] _[r]_[+1] ) _is_ = 2 _[−]_[1] 3 _[−][r]_ , which gives an exponential decay of the node dependence on input features at distance _r_ , as also heuristically argued by Alon & Yahav (2021).
comments.explanation: The qualitative point is right: in a tree-like neighborhood there is a unique shortest path from \(i\) to \(s\), and the normalized adjacency weights make the influence decay exponentially with distance. However, the specific numerical factor seems not to match the stated graph structure under the paper’s earlier definition \(\hat A=\tilde D^{-1/2}\tilde A\tilde D^{-1/2}\), with self-loops included. For a path of length \(r+1\), \((\hat A^{r+1})_{is}\) is the product of edge weights along the unique geodesic, since no self-loop step can be used in exactly \(r+1\) steps. The value \(2^{-1}3^{-r}\) is what one obtains for an augmented normalized path with endpoint augmented degrees \(2\) and internal augmented degrees \(3\), not for a binary tree. In a rooted binary tree, the root and internal vertices have different degrees, e.g. augmented degrees typically \(3\) and \(4\), giving a different product; if the terminal vertex is a leaf, that changes the last factor again. The main exponential-decay conclusion survives, but the displayed coefficient/base appears inconsistent with the example as written unless “binary tree” is intended to mean a simple path-like induced subgraph.

comments.title: Ricci curvature motivation conflates Ricci with sectional curvature/geodesic deviation
comments.quote: A natural object in Riemannian geometry is the _Ricci curvature_ , a bilinear form determining the geodesic dispersion, i.e. whether geodesics starting at nearby points with ‘same’ velocity remain parallel ( _Euclidean space_ ), converge ( _spherical space_ ), or diverge ( _hyperbolic space_ ).
comments.explanation: I understand this as an intuitive motivation for graph Ricci curvature, and the Euclidean/spherical/hyperbolic trichotomy is a useful heuristic for the later triangle/grid/tree examples. Still, strictly speaking, the behavior of nearby geodesics in a specified two-dimensional direction is governed by sectional curvature via the Jacobi equation; Ricci curvature is a trace/average of sectional curvatures and controls volume distortion or averaged focusing rather than determining pairwise geodesic dispersion in full generality. In two-dimensional constant-curvature examples the distinction collapses, so the intuition is not fatal, but the sentence overstates what Ricci curvature itself determines.

comments.title: Qualitative sign description of Balanced Forman curvature may overstate the definition
comments.quote: The curvature is negative when _i ∼ j_ behaves as a _bridge_ between _S_ 1( _i_ ) and _S_ 1( _j_ ), while it is positive when _S_ 1( _i_ ) and _S_ 1( _j_ ) stay connected after removing _i ∼ j_ .
comments.explanation: This sentence captures the intended geometric intuition: unsupported, tree-like edges should be negatively curved, while triangles and local cycles should increase curvature. However, as stated it sounds like a fairly crisp graph-theoretic characterization of the sign. From the surrounding definitions, Balanced Forman curvature depends on degrees, triangle counts, 4-cycle contributions, and the degeneracy factor \(\gamma_{\max}\), and the definition also explicitly sets \(\mathrm{Ric}(i,j)=0\) when \(\min\{d_i,d_j\}=1\). Thus a literal bridge incident to a leaf is not negative under the given definition. Conversely, global connectivity of \(S_1(i)\) and \(S_1(j)\) after deleting \(i\sim j\) need not by itself imply positive curvature unless it is realized through the local triangle/4-cycle structures counted by the formula. The prose is acceptable as intuition, but a careful reader could misread it as an exact sign criterion.

comments.title: Cheeger inequality direction in Proposition 5 appears reversed
comments.quote: **Proposition 5.** _If_ Ric( _i, j_ ) _≥ k >_ 0 _for all i ∼ j, then λ_ 1 _/_ 2 _≥ hG ≥[k]_ 2 _[.]_
comments.explanation: The intended use of curvature here is clear: Theorem 2 gives a lower bound on Ollivier curvature, Lin et al. give a spectral-gap lower bound, and Cheeger’s inequality should then turn this into a lower bound on the Cheeger constant. However, with the Cheeger constant just defined as an edge boundary divided by volume, the standard normalized-Laplacian Cheeger inequality is usually \(\lambda_1/2 \le h_G \le \sqrt{2\lambda_1}\), not \(\lambda_1/2 \ge h_G\). Combining \(\lambda_1 \ge k\) with Cheeger gives \(h_G \ge \lambda_1/2 \ge k/2\), or at least the lower bound \(h_G \ge k/2\). The displayed inequality as written would be false for standard examples such as complete graphs: for \(K_5\), \(\lambda_1/2=5/8\) while \(h_G=3/4\) under the stated definition. This looks like a genuine sign/order error rather than an OCR issue, because the subsequent prose relies on a lower bound for \(h_G\), not an upper bound.

comments.title: Bounded-gradient assumption is overstated as automatic
comments.quote: Condition (i) is always satisfied and allows us to control the message passing functions.
comments.explanation: I understand the authors’ intent: for many fixed neural-network layers used in practice, especially with Lipschitz activations and finite weights, one can obtain some finite Lipschitz/gradient bound on the relevant domain, and this is the technical condition needed to apply the Jacobian estimates. But as stated, condition (i), \(|\nabla \phi_\ell|\le \alpha\) and \(|\nabla \psi_\ell|\le \beta\), is not automatically satisfied by an arbitrary differentiable MPNN. Differentiability alone does not imply globally bounded derivatives; common mathematical counterexamples include polynomial or exponential maps, and even standard architectures need assumptions about activations, weights, and possibly bounded feature domains. The theorem can still be correct under the stated hypothesis, but the prose should say that the condition is mild or satisfied by many standard Lipschitz architectures, not that it is always satisfied.

comments.title: The causal wording about negatively curved edges is stronger than Theorem 4 establishes
comments.quote: This leads to one of the main results of this paper: **negatively curved edges are those causing the graph bottleneck and thus leading to the over-squashing phenomenon:**
comments.explanation: The surrounding discussion correctly connects the local curvature condition in Theorem 4 to small Jacobian sensitivities, and the theorem is a useful sufficient mechanism for over-squashing near strongly negative edges. However, the wording “are those causing” can be read as a necessity-and-sufficiency statement: that every bottleneck is caused by negatively curved edges, or that every negatively curved edge causes over-squashing. The theorem as described is more qualified: it requires the curvature to be negative enough relative to the degrees, includes an additional condition on \(\gamma_{\max}\), and concludes the existence of many two-hop nodes with small average sensitivity. It does not rule out other causes of over-squashing, nor does it show that all negatively curved edges necessarily produce the claimed bottleneck effect. This is mainly a framing overstatement, but a careful reader could be misled about the logical strength of the result.

comments.title: SDRF candidate edge set is underspecified for a simple graph
comments.quote: Calculate vector _**x**_ where _xkl_ = Ric _kl_ ( _i, j_ ) _−_ Ric( _i, j_ ), the improvement to Ric( _i, j_ ) from adding edge _k ∼ l_ where _k ∈ B_ 1( _i_ ), _l ∈ B_ 1( _j_ ); Sample index _k, l_ with probability softmax( _τ_ _**x**_ ) _kl_ and add edge _k ∼ l_ to _G_ .
comments.explanation: The local idea is sensible: add an edge near the most negatively curved edge to create triangle or square support. But because the graph is assumed simple, the algorithm needs to specify the admissible candidate pairs more precisely. As written, \(k\in B_1(i)\), \(l\in B_1(j)\) may include \((k,l)=(i,j)\), already-adjacent pairs, or even \(k=l\) when the neighborhoods overlap. Adding such a pair would either create a duplicate edge, a self-loop, or no topological change, which conflicts with the stated simple-graph setting and with the claim that the step adds an extra 3- or 4-cycle. The prose suggests the intended candidate set is probably nonedges with distinct endpoints, possibly excluding trivial choices involving the already existing edge, but this is not made explicit enough to reproduce the method unambiguously.

comments.title: SDRF is not specified for the directed datasets despite the theory being undirected
comments.quote: We compared the performance on graphs with no preprocessing, making the graph undirected, +FA, DIGL, SDRF, and the given combinations. For DIGL + Undirected we symmetrized the diffusion matrix as in Klicpera et al. (2019), and for SDRF + Undirected we made the graph undirected before applying SDRF.
comments.explanation: The theoretical development of Balanced Forman curvature and SDRF earlier in the paper is explicitly for simple, undirected graphs. I initially wondered whether all datasets in Table 2 were being treated as undirected by default, which would resolve the issue. But the presence of separate “Undirected” and “SDRF + Undirected” rows for the WebKB/Wikipedia/Actor-style datasets suggests that the plain SDRF row is applied before symmetrizing the graph. That leaves an important methodological gap: it is unclear how Ricci curvature is computed on directed edges, whether candidate SDRF additions are directed or undirected, and how edge removals are handled when only one direction exists. If SDRF is instead computed on the underlying undirected graph but message passing remains directed, that should also be stated. Without this clarification, the experimental comparison is hard to reproduce and is not fully aligned with the paper’s formal assumptions.

comments.title: Curvature-based rewiring is claimed not to increase cross-label connectivity without support
comments.quote: Conversely, on a low-homophily dataset, a curvature-based approach as SDRF modifies the edge set mainly around the most negatively curved edges, meaning that it decreases the bottleneck without significantly increasing the connectivity among nodes with different labels.
comments.explanation: The first part is consistent with the algorithm: SDRF targets the most negatively curved edge and makes local additions around it. What does not follow from the definitions or the preceding theory is the label-related conclusion. SDRF is label-agnostic, and in a low-homophily graph the local neighborhoods around a negatively curved edge may themselves contain many cross-class adjacencies. Adding a 3- or 4-cycle around such an edge could easily add edges between different labels. The paper may empirically observe that SDRF does not harm performance much, but that is weaker than showing that it avoids increasing inter-class connectivity. This is a plausible intuition, but as written the “meaning that” overstates what the curvature argument establishes.

comments.title: Low homophily is treated as evidence of long-range dependencies
comments.quote: In fact, long-range dependencies are often more relevant in low-homophily settings, where nodes sharing the same labels are in general not neighbors. This observation is largely confirmed by experimental results reported in the next section.
comments.explanation: I understand the intended intuition: if same-label nodes are not immediate neighbors, useful information may need to travel farther. However, low homophily does not by itself imply that the task depends on long-range same-class interactions. Many heterophilic benchmarks are often modeled through local structural patterns or class-disassortative neighbor information, not necessarily through long-range dependencies. The experimental results also do not directly measure long-range dependence; they only report node-classification accuracy after rewiring. Thus the statement is directionally plausible but somewhat stronger than what is established by either the theory or the reported experiments.

comments.title: Evaluation protocol is ambiguous around data splits, test use, and bootstrapped intervals
comments.quote: Following Shchur et al. (2018) and Klicpera et al. (2019) we optimized hyperparameters for all dataset-preprocessing combinations separately by random search over 100 data splits. Results are reported as average accuracies on a test set used once with 95% confidence intervals calculated by bootstrapping.
comments.explanation: The authors are clearly trying to follow established GNN benchmarking practice and to avoid repeated test-set tuning. Still, the wording leaves a careful reader unsure what the sampling unit is. If hyperparameters are optimized over 100 random splits, are the reported averages over those same splits, over independent retrainings on one final split, or over bootstrap resamples of nodes in a single test set? If the confidence intervals are bootstrapped over test nodes in a transductive graph, the usual independence assumptions are questionable; if they are bootstrapped over splits or runs, that should be stated. Since the table’s conclusions compare relatively small accuracy differences in several places, the exact evaluation protocol matters.

comments.title: Claim that SDRF adds and removes similar numbers of edges is not uniformly supported by Table 3
comments.quote: In comparison, SDRF adds and removes a similar number of edges and approximately preserves the degree distribution.
comments.explanation: The qualitative comparison with DIGL is fair: Table 3 shows that DIGL often densifies the graph by hundreds or thousands of percent, whereas SDRF changes far fewer edges. However, the stronger statement that SDRF adds and removes a similar number of edges is not true across all reported datasets. For Cornell the table gives 7.8% added versus 33.3% removed; for Texas, 2.4% versus 10.4%; for Wisconsin, 1.4% versus 7.5%; and for Actor, 5.4% versus 9.9%. On several larger datasets the percentages are indeed almost equal, so the point is not wholly wrong, but the prose overgeneralizes relative to the displayed numbers.

comments.title: Observed Jacobian increases are over-attributed to Theorem 4’s upper bound
comments.quote: network’s Jacobian between a node’s prediction and the features of its 2-hop neighbors (encoded as node colors) are increased over the graph, which we may attribute to both DIGL and SDRF’s ability to alleviate the upper bound presented in Theorem 4 and thus reduce over-squashing.
comments.explanation: It is reasonable to visualize Jacobians, since the paper has used Jacobian sensitivity as a proxy for over-squashing. The concern is the causal interpretation. Theorem 4 gives a sufficient curvature-based upper-bound statement for certain 2-hop sensitivities around sufficiently negatively curved edges. Observing larger trained Jacobians after preprocessing does not by itself show that the upper bound was the active constraint, nor that the increase is specifically caused by the mechanism in Theorem 4 rather than by changed normalization, retraining dynamics, altered degrees, or other graph changes. The phrase “may attribute” is cautious, but the connection to “thus reduce over-squashing” is still stronger than the displayed visualization alone can justify.

comments.title: Theorem 4 is summarized more strongly than its stated hypotheses support
comments.quote: We then proved in Theorem 4 that negatively-curved edges are responsible for over-squashing, calling for a possibility of curvature-based rewiring of the graph in order to improve its bottleneckedness.
comments.explanation: The high-level intent is clear: the paper’s main conceptual contribution is to connect negative local curvature with bottleneck-like message compression, and Theorem 4 is indeed the formal result supporting this narrative. However, checking the theorem statement against this summary, the result is more conditional and quantitative than the prose suggests. Theorem 4 applies to an edge satisfying a sufficiently strong negative-curvature condition relative to the degrees, with bounded message/update derivatives and an additional non-degeneracy condition on \(\gamma_{\max}\). It then gives the existence of a set of distance-two nodes for which an average Jacobian bound is small. That is good evidence that certain strongly negatively curved edges induce local over-squashing, but it does not prove that all negatively curved edges are responsible for over-squashing, nor that negative curvature is necessary for over-squashing in every graph/task setting. Because this is the conclusion, some compression is acceptable, but the wording 'proved ... negatively-curved edges are responsible' overstates the theorem unless qualified as 'sufficiently negatively curved edges, under the stated assumptions'.

comments.title: The claimed theoretical advantage of SDRF over diffusion rewiring is broader than what appears to be proved
comments.quote: We show both theoretically and experimentally that the proposed method can be advantageous compared to a diffusion-based rewiring approach, opening the door for curvature-based rewiring methods for improving GNN performance going forward.
comments.explanation: The authors’ intended contrast is understandable: SDRF targets negatively curved edges identified by the theory, while the paper argues that diffusion/PPR-style rewiring may fail to improve bottleneck measures such as Cheeger ratios and may behave like denoising rather than bottleneck repair. The experimental part can support that SDRF is sometimes advantageous. What is less clearly supported is the 'theoretically' part as applied to the proposed method itself. From the surrounding context, the formal theory establishes that negative curvature can create local Jacobian bottlenecks and that diffusion-based rewiring can fail to improve certain Cheeger-type quantities. Algorithm 1 is then motivated by this theory, but there does not appear to be a formal guarantee that SDRF improves the Cheeger constant, improves curvature globally, reduces the Jacobian bound, or outperforms diffusion rewiring under specified conditions. Thus the theoretical result seems to justify SDRF’s design and expose a limitation of diffusion methods, rather than directly prove SDRF’s advantage. A more precise phrasing would separate these: theoretically motivated by the curvature results and empirically advantageous over diffusion baselines.
